food_subgroup: Crustaceans — cooked slipper lobster in butter, cheese, and cream sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for slipper lobster (Thenus orientalis) prepared with butter and cheese sauce. FOODON_03302757 (lobster food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (Lobster) is the closest parent. No specific FoodEx2 entry exists for slipper lobster prepared with butter-cheese cream sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227261009 represents lobster as a substance. No specific code exists for this prepared dish.

serving.description: 1 portion (shared dish, approximately 300 g per person)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Creamy Butter Cheese Slipper Lobster is approximately 300 g per person, representing one portion of a shared Zi Char seafood dish.

nutrient_source.title: Butter-sauce crustacean dish — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: Lobster cooked (FDC ID 175180), Butter salted (FDC ID 173410), Processed cheese (FDC ID 172185), and Evaporated milk (FDC ID 170877); proportions estimated from standard Malaysian Zi Char butter lobster recipe
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central for creamy butter cheese slipper lobster as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis: approximately 55% cooked lobster meat, 15% butter, 15% processed cheese, 10% evaporated milk, and 5% aromatics (garlic, curry leaves, chilli). Values represent the edible portion per 100 g of the finished dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — shellfish and high-protein low-carbohydrate foods; analogous estimate based on cooked lobster and butter-cream sauce components
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for creamy butter cheese slipper lobster. GI is estimated at 25 based on the dominant macronutrient profile: high protein (14.5 g/100 g), high fat (13.8 g/100 g), and very low carbohydrate (3.2 g/100 g). Shellfish and high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate cooked dishes consistently show GI values in the 20–30 range per University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (25 × 3.2 × 3) / 100 = 2.4, classified as very low. gi_category set to 'low' per threshold rule (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~1260 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less, as a standard 300 g serving provides approximately 1260 mg sodium — over half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — primarily from butter, processed cheese, and added salt in the sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.2 g/100 g) and low glycemic index (~25)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Creamy Butter Cheese Slipper Lobster has a very low glycemic index (~25) and minimal carbohydrate load (approximately 9.6 g per 300 g serving), making it unlikely to cause significant postprandial glucose excursions; however, the high saturated fat content warrants attention for individuals with concurrent dyslipidaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (8.2 g/100 g) and cholesterol (145 mg/100 g) from butter and cheese
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The substantial saturated fat (~24.6 g per 300 g serving) and dietary cholesterol (~435 mg per 300 g serving) from butter and processed cheese may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in small portions, preferring preparations with reduced butter and cheese.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Creamy Butter Cheese Slipper Lobster is a rich Malaysian-Singaporean Zi Char seafood dish featuring slipper lobster (Thenus orientalis, also known as flathead lobster or Balmain bug) cooked in a luxurious sauce of butter, processed cheese, evaporated milk or cream, egg yolk, garlic, curry leaves, and bird's eye chilli. The dish is prepared by wok-frying the halved slipper lobster in hot butter until the shell turns bright orange, then coating the meat in a creamy, savoury-sweet cheese sauce that clings to the shell and flesh. The texture is succulent and tender with a rich, umami-laden coating that balances the natural sweetness of the crustacean against the saltiness of the cheese and the fragrance of curry leaves. Slipper lobsters are smaller and flatter than spiny lobsters, with a broad, fan-shaped tail that yields sweet, firm white meat. This dish is a signature offering at Zi Char restaurants — informal Chinese-style seafood eateries common in Singapore and Malaysia — and is typically ordered as a shared centrepiece dish alongside rice and other stir-fried vegetables. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~14.5 g/100 g) and fat (~13.8 g/100 g, predominantly saturated from dairy), with very low carbohydrate content (~3.2 g/100 g) and a low glycemic index (~25). The sodium content is elevated (~420 mg/100 g) due to butter, cheese, and seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is a good source of vitamin B12, selenium, zinc, and phosphorus from the lobster component, and provides calcium from the dairy ingredients.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for creamy butter cheese slipper lobster exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked lobster, butter, processed cheese, and evaporated milk using standard Zi Char recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on macronutrient profile. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central (cooked lobster FDC 175180, salted butter FDC 173410, processed cheese FDC 172185, evaporated milk FDC 170877) using approximate recipe proportions: 55% lobster meat, 15% butter, 15% processed cheese, 10% evaporated milk, 5% aromatics. These should be replaced with direct laboratory analysis or an approved primary source entry if one becomes available. 2. Slipper lobster (Thenus orientalis) is distinct from spiny lobster (Panulirus spp.) and clawed lobster (Homarus spp.); it is widely available in Southeast Asian seafood markets and Zi Char restaurants. 3. GI is estimated at 25 based on analogous high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate shellfish dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in peer-reviewed literature. 4. Sodium content may vary significantly between restaurants depending on the quantity of butter, cheese, and added salt used. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (3.6), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.8), trans_fat_g (0.3), potassium_mg (210.0), calcium_mg (95.0), iron_mg (0.6), cholesterol_mg (145.0), vitamin_c_mg (1.2), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (95.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.05), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.09), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (2.1), vitamin_b6_mg (0.12), vitamin_b12_mcg (1.8), folate_mcg_dfe (12.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.4), vitamin_e_mg (0.9), phosphorus_mg (185.0), magnesium_mg (28.0), zinc_mg (2.4), selenium_mcg (38.0), and water_g (62.0) are all estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not confirmed by a direct primary source measurement for this dish. 6. The dish is classified as halal-compatible as slipper lobster is a crustacean permissible under most Malay-Muslim dietary interpretations, and no pork or alcohol is used in the standard preparation; however, halal certification status of individual restaurants should be verified independently. 7. Dietary fibre_g (0.1) reflects negligible fibre from aromatics; this is a confirmed near-zero value from component analysis, not a data gap.
